utterances: (Soundbite of laughter)
You know, there's this wonderful old clipping from the New York Times from 1966 - article written by Craig Claiborne.
Oh, yes.
The headline is: Indian Actress is a Star in the Kitchen Too.
Right.
You know, what did you think when you found yourself becoming famous as a cook and a food writer?
I had trouble with that, and I have had trouble taking it seriously.
But I consider both of them to be my professions now.
But one was the one I sought and studied for, and the other dropped from heaven.
We have tape that we'd just like to play here...
Uh-oh.
...from the cooking series you did for the BBC.
This one is 1982.
Oh gosh, that was the first one, yeah.
Madhur Jaffrey's Indian Cookery.
(Soundbite of TV show, ""Madhur Jaffrey's Indian Cookery"")
In this series, I'm going to show you how to cook all kinds of Indian food.
Some of it, you may be familiar with - the kind of food you've had in Indian restaurants - and some of it, you've probably never eaten.
It's the kind of food you get in Indian homes.
One of the things I'm going to make it Rogan Josh.
This is a classical North Indian dish.
I'm also going to show you how to cook cauliflower, potatoes...
I'm hungry.
(Soundbite of laughter)
Me, too.
You know, hearing this tape now, nearly 30 years later, did you know what you had gotten yourself into?
Not at all.
I was told that this was an educational program.
And this was the first time that British people were actually cooking proper Indian food in their homes.
And I remember reading articles like, they've run out of cilantro - which is called green coriander - in Manchester because I had made a dish the day before.
And it really changed the way the English were eating Indian food before.
There was very little of it, for one thing, and whatever there was at that time was very, very cheap.
It was considered cheap, spicy food to go with beer.
That's what the association was.
One last question: When we speak with musicians here on this program, we often ask them to suggest a song for us to go out on.
Is there a recipe...
A dish to go out on?
...a dish that you would like to share with us, to go out on?
Yes, there is a recipe that I might suggest.
And that is a recipe that is absolutely wonderful in the season.
And it's a red-pepper soup with ginger and fennel.
This is a good time to have it and keep your sinuses clear.
Thank you very much for joining us.
Thank you.
(Soundbite of music)
You'll find that recipe at our website, NPR.org, and in the new cookbook ""At Home with Madhur Jaffrey."" Tomorrow, ""The Long View"" Italian style, with Chef Marcella Hazan.
This is NPR News.
But as NPR's Jim Zarroli reports, other employees at the airline will also vote soon on whether to join unions.
Nelson Lichtenstein, a professor of history at the University of California at Santa Barbara, says other employee groups are voting whether to unionize in coming weeks.
And if they succeed, it could raise support for a union at Delta.
If the machinists and the ground personnel vote in favor of the union in the next election, then a couple of years down the line, the flight attendants may have another election, and they would join.
That's happened in the past.
Jim Zarroli, NPR News.
It's WEEKENDS on ALL THINGS CONSIDERED from NPR News.
I'm Guy Raz.
There's a new stimulus plan under way in America as we speak - $5.8 billion being injected into the U.S.
economy, particularly in states like Ohio and Virginia and Colorado and Florida.
You might have guessed that I'm talking about campaign spending.
Our cover story today: Is the money game having any effect so far?
(SOUNDBITE OF MUSIC)
Much of what Canada exports are commodities.
Most of those commodities are priced in U.S.
dollars.
Ah, okay.
Today, we've got around 50,000 hits in this particular collection.
And that's a particularly good result, because we estimate there are around 500,000 people with Barbadian ancestry.
So it's a good 10 percent of that population has gone on and had a look.
How expensive a proposition, Simon, is this project?
And who pays for it?
It's not an expensive proposition for us to put online.
When this entire collection is complete, it will be around 3 million names in total.
But it is a very important collection, particularly to those interested in researching slave ancestry or black family history.
In terms of who pays for it, we are a membership-base so you can take out a variety of different memberships depending on your needs.
What has been your experience so far with regard to people who have conducted searches through the database?
Once people find out and trace their lineage, what do they tend to do with that information?
Well, people do a variety of things with the information that they discover.
Some people just keep it for their own personal enjoyment, or they share it with their families.
Ancestral tourism is a travel trend on the up.
People are going and visiting where their ancestors came from, where they lived, where they grew up and even where they worked.
People are doing a variety of different things.
Social networking is a great (unintelligible) of this particular practice.
People are meeting second, third, fourth cousins through their family history research.

Look ahead for us and talk about how far into history you would intend to go with other registers of this sort, and how widespread globally, with regard to the slave trade, something like this is likely to develop into?

We anticipate to have the entire 700 registers, which cover the 23 colonial dependencies and British territories who submitted registers between 1812 and 1834, online within the next nine to 12 months.
So that three million names will be of incredible significance to anyone who has ancestry relating to any of those different parts of the world.
And of course, after slavery was abolished in 1834, former slaves traveled all over the world and particularly to other parts of the British Empire.
So for many, many people out there, they will have ancestors contained in these collections so there will be a lot of relevance.
There's one more thing that just came to mind, Simon, and I'm also curious about.
When we talk about slave history and we think of blacks both in the U.K.
as well as in America and other places who are trying to trace their ancestry, but there are slave owners and ancestors of slave owners who might also be interested in a database like this.
Do you find that those people are also interested in pursuing this?
Yes, absolutely.
In the Barbados register that we launched there are 5,000 names alone just pertaining to slave owners.
So I'm not, at this stage, sure of how many names will be in the overall collection when it's online, but there must be around maybe 50,000 owners.
And so for those people, yeah, absolutely.
Family history is of interest to anyone who is researching their family history, so it will be of just as much relevance to those who owned the slaves as it will be to the former slaves.
Simon, thank you very much.
This is very interesting.
Thank you.
